summary: Frazelle reflects on trust and integrity in leadership after spending time observing the finance industry. She argues that transparency and trust go hand-in-hand, and leaders must earn trust through a track record of doing the right thing rather than assuming it will be given. She criticizes leaders who weaponize information asymmetry against their reports, and emphasizes that unlike respect, trust starts at zero and must be actively built.
key_points: Trust and transparency are inseparable leadership qualities
Leaders often weaponize information asymmetry against their teams
Trust starts at zero and must be earned, unlike respect
Bad experiences teach you to read people and protect yourself
'Assume good intentions' has limits based on lived experience
money_quotes: I became better at reading people after having a few really bad experiences where I should have known better than to trust someone.

Most people will tell you to always assume good intentions, and this is true to an extent. However, having been through some really bad experiences where I did 'assume good intentions' and should not have, I tend to be less and less willing to do that.
I've seen a lot of people in positions of power, use their power of keeping information private against those under them.
They don't fully disclose the 'why' and it leads to people they manage not fully being able to help solve the problem as well as not fully understanding the problem.
To gain trust, I believe a leader must also have integrity and a track record of doing the right thing.
key_insight: Trust is not given but earned through consistent integrity, and leaders who default to secrecy over transparency are often weaponizing information asymmetry against their own teams.
